frozen_components: Host SAE weights (during stitching and meta-SAE training)
architecture.summary: The paper introduces two analysis techniques for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on LLM activations. SAE stitching swaps latent directions between SAEs of different dictionary sizes, measuring reconstruction performance changes to classify latents as novel (capturing new information) or reconstruction (refining existing features). Meta-SAEs are SAEs trained on the decoder directions of a larger SAE to decompose those directions into interpretable sparse combinations of meta-latents, revealing compositional structure. Information flows through decoder direction similarity metrics (cosine similarity thresholds) and reconstruction loss measurements.
trained_components: Meta-SAE encoder and decoder trained on decoder directions of host SAE

biblio.title: Sparse Autoencoders Do Not Find Canonical Units of Analysis

claims.statement: Smaller SAEs are incomplete: larger SAEs learn novel latents that improve performance when added, indicating they capture information missed by smaller SAEs
Larger SAE latents are not atomic but often decompose into combinations of latents from smaller SAEs due to the sparsity penalty incentivizing composition of features
Meta-SAE trained on GPT-2 SAE decoder directions (dictionary size 49152) explains 55.47% of variance of the decoder directions

claims.dataset: GPT-2 Small SAE with dictionary size 49152
claims.statement: Meta-SAE latent explanations evaluated in zero-shot multiple-choice: GPT-4o-mini chose the correct SAE latent explanation 73% of the time among 5 options on 1,000 sampled latents
Example latent decompositions show interpretability: a latent representing 'Einstein' decomposes into meta-latents for 'scientist', 'Germany', 'famous person', and other related concepts

SAEs do not find a canonical set of units: rather than converging on a unique, complete, and irreducible set of features, SAEs of different sizes offer varying granularities and compositions of features
The paper acknowledges its work only studies two LLMs (GPT-2 Small and Gemma 2 2B) and does not include very large SAEs such as in Templeton (2024)

Meta-SAE latents show high maximum cosine similarity to latents from SAEs of comparable size trained directly on network activations, supporting the hypothesis that larger SAE latents are compositions of features from smaller SAEs
